title: Do AI-driven strategies actually provide an edge in crypto, or are we fooling ourselves?
author: selimkurnaz
selftext:
I've been working on ML-based meta-label classifiers for a while, layering them on top of trend-following and mean-reversion setups and running backtests. The results sometimes look promising, and sometimes good enough to make me think "is this just overfit?", you know how it goes.

What I'm curious about: is anyone here actually live-trading with AI/ML? Do you see a meaningful difference compared to classic TA strategies, or does most of the edge still come down to execution and risk management? And in practice, how much do you really need to watch out for things like label leakage and regime change?

The reason I'm asking specifically about crypto is that the 24/7 market and the abundance of on-chain data feels like a decent playground for AI, but then again the noise level is pretty high too. Would love to hear your experiences.

comments.author: PleasantSomewhere990
comments.body: Feels like the real test is whether it survives walk-forward and regime shifts, not just a clean backtest

replies.author: selimkurnaz
replies.body: Yeah, totally agree, that’s exactly where it gets tricky. I can manage the backtesting side pretty well with ML, but in forward testing it really doesn’t hold up long-term like you said. The moment there’s data drift, the model’s accuracy starts shifting too. I’m trying to build a fully automated model-retraining pipeline to handle this, but it’s a tough problem to crack honestly. Thanks for bringing this up.

comments.author: purplethunder383
comments.body:
From what I have seen, AI can add a real edge, but usually in narrower ways than people expect. Most of the lift comes from filtering trades, sizing risk, or adapting thresholds across regimes rather than discovering entirely new signals. When it looks amazing in backtests, overfitting or leakage is almost always lurking somewhere.

In live crypto trading, execution, fees, latency, and risk management still dominate outcomes. ML helps most when it acts as a decision layer on top of simpler strategies instead of trying to predict direction directly. Regime shifts are the hardest part, and models that are retrained often or explicitly regime aware tend to survive longer.

Crypto is a great playground because of data volume and structure, but the noise is brutal. In practice, AI feels more like a consistency and discipline tool than a magic alpha generator.

replies.author: selimkurnaz
replies.body:
This matches my experience pretty closely. The "decision layer on top of a simple strategy" framing is exactly where I've landed too — trying to use ML to predict direction from scratch is where most people (me included, early on) burn cycles for nothing. Filtering and sizing is where the lift actually shows up.

Agree on the retrain cadence too. A static model in crypto ages fast. The harder question for me is *how* often to retrain without overfitting to recent noise — too frequent and you're chasing, too rare and you're stuck in a dead regime. Still figuring out the right window.

comments.author: Zestyclose-Eagle1809
comments.body:
Yes, ML edge in crypto is real but narrower than the backtests suggest, and the failure mode is more specific than generic overfitting.

The trap with meta-labeling specifically: your secondary classifier learns from the primary's trade history, which means it inherits the primary's selection bias. If your trend-following primary fired more in 2021-2022 trending regimes, the meta-label classifier learns "this looks like a trade I should size up" from a sample that's structurally biased toward those conditions. When the regime shifts, the meta-label confidence stays high while the actual edge has already evaporated. That's not overfit, it's a more subtle problem of training on a non-representative sample of your own decisions....

Label leakage in crypto is brutal because of the 24/7 nature. There's no overnight gap to enforce a clean split.. If you're labeling trades with forward returns and your features include rolling stats that don't strictly stop at the trade entry, you'll leak future information without realizing it. The classic check: train on data through date X, predict on date X+1, and verify your features at prediction time only use data available before X+1. Most ML pipelines fail this and the bug is invisible from the equity curve.

Regime change is the one you can't fully solve, only manage. The realistic test: walk-forward through at least 2-3 distinct volatility regimes and look at whether expectancy holds in each separately, not blended. A model that's 1.5 Sharpe overall but -0.3 in the bear regime isn't an edge, it's a long-vol bet with extra steps.

What's your meta-label target? sizing, or filtering trades the primary already greenlit? They fail differently.

replies.author: selimkurnaz
replies.body:
Thanks, this is genuinely one of the more useful comments I've gotten on this — you put your finger on the exact thing that's been nagging me.

The inherited selection bias point lands hard. My primary fires on a MACD cross + RSI window + ADX filter, and I went back and looked at the temporal distribution of those signals across my training window. It's exactly what you'd predict: heavily skewed toward trending stretches. So my meta-label classifier is learning "what does a successful trade look like" from a sample that's structurally biased toward conditions where the primary works in the first place. Confidence stays high after the regime turns because the model has never really *seen* what a degraded primary looks like — it's been trained on the primary's good days.

On the use case: I'm running it as a filter, not for sizing. Primary greenlights, secondary decides take/skip with a confidence threshold. Which is the worse of the two failure modes for exactly the reason you said — sizing degrades gracefully when confidence is miscalibrated, but a filter that says "take" on a biased sample just compounds the problem at the worst possible time.

What I have on the diagnostics side is honestly pretty thin against what you're describing. I track Brier score and reliability bins on the test set, plus a precision-uplift-over-baseline sweep across thresholds. So I can see *after the fact* when predicted probability and actual success rate diverge. But it's all aggregate. Nothing breaking it down by volatility regime, nothing telling me "your 0.78 threshold is fine in mid-vol but garbage in high-vol." So the model could be 1.5 Sharpe blended and -0.3 in one regime and I just wouldn't know from my current eval output. That's the gap your comment surfaced for me.

The fixes I'm thinking about: partition the test set by some regime proxy (ATR percentile bucket, or a HMM state if I want to get fancier) and rerun calibration + threshold sweep per bucket. Probably also need to look at the training sample's regime distribution and consider reweighting or stratified resampling so the classifier isn't dominated by one regime's examples. Walk-forward I do in a loose way through rolling retrains, but you're right that the discipline of explicitly checking "does expectancy hold in each regime separately" is different from just letting time advance.

On the leakage side — yeah, the 24/7 thing is exactly why I'm paranoid about rolling stats. I'm enforcing strict horizon-based labels (forward window stops at i + horizon, features computed on data ≤ i, purged splits with embargo). I think it's clean but I haven't done the train-through-X / predict-X+1 audit you described as a deliberate check. Adding it to the list.

The "long-vol bet with extra steps" framing is going to live in my head rent-free. Thanks for the comment.

replies.author: Zestyclose-Eagle1809
replies.body:
The filter-not-sizing detail actually sharpens the risk. A miscalibrated sizing model degrades smoothly, you bet a bit too big or small.

Your fix list is right, but there's a trap in "rerun calibration + threshold sweep per bucket" worth naming before you build it. The moment you fit a separate threshold per regime, you've multiplied your free parameters, one threshold becomes three or four while shrinking the sample each one is fit on. That's a classic way to trade one overfitting problem for another: your per-regime thresholds will look great in-sample because you've given the model more degrees of freedom to fit noise in each bucket.

The more robust version: use the regime split to diagnose and validate, not to fit. Check whether a single global threshold holds its precision-uplift across all regime buckets. If it does, you have a genuinely regime-robust filter and you keep one parameter. If it collapses in high-vol, that's not a signal to fit a high-vol-specific threshold, it's a signal the filter has no edge in high-vol and you should stand the system down there entirely, not paper over it with a tuned number. Standing down beats a fragile per-regime fit.

This whole class of problem, "is my edge real per-regime or am I fitting my own eval" is what we ended up building Quantprove around. We kept finding models that looked clean on aggregate diagnostics and fell apart once we forced regime-segmented, multiple-testing-corrected eval. Co-Founder, so I'm biased, but the regime-robustness-vs-per-regime-fit distinction is the exact thing it's meant to make visible. Happy to go deeper on the methodology either way.

Did your precision-uplift sweep use the same data to pick the threshold and report the uplift? If so, that number's optimistic regardless of regime, and that's worth fixing before the regime partitioning, because it's upstream of everything else.

comments.author: Spirited_Syllabub488
comments.body:
Hey guys I’ve my own ML trading system for BTC and Gold that I’ve been working on for a while.

It’s been backtested over 5 years and has averaged around 10% per month on fixed lot, with Sharpe above 3. And it's been love for around 8 months now.

I don’t currently have the capital to scale it myself, so I’m open to speaking with someone serious who may be interested in acquiring it.

If anyone is really interested to see the report and the live trades data just hit me up.

comments.author: Choice_Run1329
comments.body:
Regime change is the real killer, not overfitting. I've blown up two backtests that looked incredible on 2021-2023 data and fell apart the second volatility structure shifted in Q1 2024. Label leakage is easier to catch if you're disciplined about purging your train/test splits, but regime detection is genuinely hard.

What helped me was running simpler ensemble models and sizing down aggressively when prediction confidence drops. For live execution I moved my crypto perps to markets xyz since 24/7 liquidity matters when your signals fire at 3am on a Sunday.

comments.author: qqAzo
comments.body:
AI sees patterns in indicators. I doubt AI will perform much better than a proper setup using indicators, levels etc. I think the real value could be setting up the framework with an AI. Using it to analyze the last 15 years of Crypto data and defining where it sees correlation.

But you'd need market breath, various strategies for various times and much more. I just built it for the stock market and I'd guess Crypto is easier as there are less moving pieces and most things move in correlation to each other.
